title: Conceptual modelling
objectives: Define conceptual modelling and explain its purpose in the design process.
Describe the main types of conceptual models used by designers.
Explain how mind maps and mood boards support the early stages of design.
Analyse the advantages of conceptual modelling over jumping directly to physical prototyping.
Discuss how conceptual models are used to communicate ideas to clients and stakeholders.
summary:
Conceptual modelling is the earliest stage of design modelling, occurring when a design is still at the level of ideas rather than physical form. It is the process of externalising and communicating abstract design concepts using low-fidelity, quickly produced representations that can be easily modified, compared, and critiqued. The primary purpose of conceptual modelling is to explore a wide design space rapidly and cheaply, before committing resources to more detailed development.

Conceptual models include a wide range of tools: annotated sketches that capture initial ideas with rough proportions and written notes; mind maps that radiate related concepts outward from a central theme, helping designers explore associations and connections; mood boards that assemble images, colours, textures, and references to establish the aesthetic direction and emotional tone of a design; and storyboards that sequence a user's interaction with a product or service over time, borrowed from film and interaction design.

Mind mapping is particularly effective during problem analysis and brief interpretation. Starting with the design problem or brief at the centre, a designer rapidly generates associated ideas, constraints, materials, user needs, and existing solutions in a non-linear, free-associative format. This helps break out of constrained thinking and often reveals unexpected connections. Mood boards perform a different function: they translate a desired user experience or brand character into a visual language that can be shared with clients and team members who may not easily interpret technical drawings. They are especially important when communicating aesthetic intent across professional boundaries.

Annotated sketches are arguably the most fundamental conceptual modelling tool. Quick, rough sketches produced with pencil or digital stylus allow designers to generate many ideas rapidly, test proportions, explore variations, and record thoughts. Annotations add context — noting materials, mechanisms, dimensions, or user interactions that cannot be read from the image alone. The process of drawing is itself a thinking tool: designers often discover problems and opportunities in their ideas while drawing that they would not have identified through verbal or written description alone.

Conceptual modelling provides significant advantages over immediately building physical prototypes. It is fast and cheap, allowing many ideas to be explored before any single approach is committed to. It is also non-prescriptive — a rough sketch does not lock in materials, dimensions, or manufacturing methods, leaving design freedom for subsequent stages. It facilitates communication and early feedback, allowing clients and users to respond to ideas before expensive development work has been done. The limitation of conceptual models is that they cannot fully test functional performance, structural integrity, or human factors — these require physical models and prototypes.

quiz.question: Define conceptual modelling and explain its role in the early stages of the design process.
quiz.markscheme: Conceptual modelling is the use of low-fidelity, quickly produced representations (sketches, mind maps, mood boards) to explore and communicate design ideas before physical form is established. Its role is to rapidly explore a wide range of ideas, identify promising directions, and facilitate early feedback from clients and stakeholders — all at low cost and without committing to specific materials or manufacturing methods.

quiz.question: Explain how a mood board differs from a mind map as a conceptual modelling tool, and describe a situation where each would be most useful.
quiz.markscheme: A mind map is a radial diagram that explores conceptual connections and associations around a central theme — most useful for problem analysis, brief interpretation, and early idea generation. A mood board assembles images, colours, and textures to define the aesthetic direction and emotional tone — most useful when communicating visual/aesthetic intent to clients, or when a team needs to align on the desired look and feel before detailed design work begins.

quiz.question: Describe the characteristics of effective annotated sketches as a design communication tool.
quiz.markscheme: Effective annotated sketches are quick and freehand, capturing the essence of an idea without requiring precision or scale. They include written notes (annotations) that explain materials, mechanisms, dimensions, user interactions, or design intent that cannot be read from the drawing alone. They may show multiple views or variants. They are used to externalise thinking, explore variations rapidly, and record ideas for later development or communication to clients and team members.

quiz.question: Analyse the advantages of conceptual modelling over immediately proceeding to physical prototyping.
quiz.markscheme: Conceptual modelling is fast and inexpensive, allowing many ideas to be explored before costly physical work begins. It does not lock in design decisions, preserving design freedom. It enables early client and user feedback, catching problems before investment is made. It can be quickly modified or discarded. Physical prototyping is time-consuming and expensive, and is better used to test a promising concept that has already been refined through conceptual modelling. Conceptual models are therefore more appropriate at the exploration stage.

quiz.question: Discuss how designers use conceptual models to communicate with clients who may not be able to read technical drawings.
quiz.markscheme: Technical drawings require specialist training to interpret. Conceptual tools such as mood boards, perspective sketches, and storyboards communicate design intent visually and intuitively, allowing non-specialist clients to understand the proposed aesthetic, user experience, and product character. This supports informed client decision-making at an early stage, reducing the risk of costly late-stage changes. Storyboards are particularly effective for communicating service or interaction design concepts, while mood boards convey brand and aesthetic direction without requiring any technical literacy.

title: Graphical modelling
objectives: Define graphical modelling and identify its main forms used in design.
Describe the conventions of orthographic (third-angle) projection drawing.
Explain the purpose and conventions of isometric and perspective drawing.
Describe how working drawings communicate manufacturing information.
Analyse the advantages and limitations of graphical modelling compared to physical models.
summary:
Graphical modelling uses two-dimensional drawn representations to communicate design intent, dimensions, and manufacturing information. It is the language through which designers communicate precise information to manufacturers, engineers, and other specialists. While conceptual models are exploratory and informal, graphical models are typically more structured, follow agreed conventions, and convey quantitative information.

Orthographic projection is the standard engineering drawing method for presenting precise dimensional information. In third-angle projection (used in the USA and many other countries), three views are drawn — front elevation, plan (top view), and side elevation — arranged in a standardised layout so that each view is adjacent to the face it shows. Dimensions, materials, tolerances, and surface finishes are annotated according to international standards (ISO). First-angle projection (common in Europe) uses the same views but arranges them differently. Students must be able to recognise and interpret both but should understand the underlying principle of projecting a 3D object onto 2D planes.

Isometric drawing presents a three-dimensional view of an object at 30° to the horizontal, with all three axes shown at equal scale — unlike true perspective, there is no foreshortening. Isometric drawings are useful for communicating the overall form and proportions of a product to non-specialist audiences. They are commonly used in assembly instructions and product catalogues. Perspective drawing, by contrast, uses one, two, or three vanishing points to create an impression of depth that more closely resembles how the human eye perceives a scene. One-point perspective is used for interior spaces and corridor views; two-point perspective is the most common for depicting objects.

Working drawings (also called production drawings or manufacturing drawings) are detailed technical drawings that provide all the information needed to manufacture a component. They include dimensioned orthographic views, material specifications, surface finish requirements, tolerances, and assembly information. A drawing number, title block, scale, and date are required. Working drawings may be produced using traditional manual drafting instruments or, far more commonly today, using computer-aided drafting (CAD) software.

Graphical modelling offers precision, reproducibility, and the ability to communicate detailed information unambiguously to manufacturers. It is the established professional language of engineering and manufacturing. Its limitations include the time and skill required to produce detailed drawings, the inability to fully communicate dynamic behaviour or user experience, and the need for specialist knowledge to interpret technical drawings. Rendered perspective drawings and isometrics partially address the communication limitation by presenting objects in a more visually intuitive way.

quiz.question: Describe the conventions of third-angle orthographic projection drawing.
quiz.markscheme: Third-angle projection presents an object in three standard views: front elevation, plan (top view), and side elevation. The views are arranged so that each view is placed adjacent to the face it depicts — the plan is above the front elevation, the right side view is to the right of the front elevation. All views are drawn to a consistent scale and are fully dimensioned. First-angle projection uses the same views but the plan is below the front elevation and the right side view is to the left — students should know both conventions exist.

quiz.question: Explain the difference between isometric drawing and perspective drawing, and describe a design situation where each would be most appropriate.
quiz.markscheme: Isometric drawing projects all three axes at equal scale at 30° to the horizontal — no foreshortening, so it is easy to draw to scale and remains dimensionally accurate. Best for assembly instructions and technical illustrations where scale is important. Perspective drawing uses vanishing points to simulate the depth perception of the human eye — more visually realistic and communicative. Best for client presentations, visualisations, and interior design where communicating the 'look and feel' to non-specialists is the priority.

quiz.question: Describe what information must be included on a working (production) drawing.
quiz.markscheme: A working drawing must include: dimensioned orthographic views (front, plan, side); material specification; surface finish requirements; tolerances (allowable dimensional variation); assembly information where relevant; a title block containing the drawing number, title, scale, date, drafter's name, and any revision history. All dimensions must be in a consistent unit (usually mm) and follow standard conventions (ISO or ANSI).

quiz.question: Analyse the advantages and limitations of graphical modelling as a method of communicating design intent.
quiz.markscheme: Advantages: precise, unambiguous dimensional communication; reproducible and scalable; the universal professional language of engineering; enables manufacturing without the designer being present; can be stored, revised, and reissued systematically. Limitations: requires specialist training to interpret (orthographic drawings are not intuitive to non-specialists); cannot communicate dynamic behaviour, user experience, or aesthetic qualities fully; time-consuming to produce manually; 2D representation of 3D objects can be difficult to visualise. Rendered perspectives partially overcome the communication limitation for non-specialists.

quiz.question: Explain how computer-aided drafting (CAD) software has changed the production and use of graphical models in industry.
quiz.markscheme: CAD software has replaced manual drafting in most industrial contexts, offering speed (parametric changes update all views automatically), accuracy (precision is limited only by input data), easy revision control, and direct digital data transfer to downstream processes (CNC machining, simulation, documentation). CAD files can be shared electronically across global supply chains instantly. CAD also enables 3D modelling alongside 2D drawing, so orthographic views can be generated automatically from a 3D model. The skill required shifts from manual drawing to 3D modelling and CAD system operation.

title: Physical modelling
objectives: Define physical modelling and explain its purpose in product development.
Describe the different types of physical models used in design, including appearance models, working models, and prototypes.
Explain how the fidelity of a physical model is chosen in relation to its purpose.
Analyse the advantages of physical models over purely graphical or digital representations.
Discuss the materials and techniques commonly used to construct physical models.
summary:
Physical modelling involves the construction of tangible, three-dimensional representations of a design. Physical models serve a range of purposes throughout the product development process: from early, rough explorations of form to highly refined pre-production prototypes that replicate virtually every aspect of the final product. The choice of model type, materials, and level of finish depends on what questions need to be answered at each stage of development.

Physical models are classified broadly by their fidelity and function. Appearance models (also called presentation models or aesthetic models) are made to look like the final product but may have no working parts. They are built to assess and communicate the visual form, proportions, colour, and surface quality of a design. They are used in client presentations, marketing photography, and focus groups. Working models demonstrate specific functional aspects of a design — how a mechanism operates, for example — but may not look like the final product. Prototypes are the most complete physical models, combining appearance and function to closely approximate the final product; they are used for user testing, engineering validation, and pre-production checks.

Fidelity refers to how closely a model replicates the final product. Low-fidelity models (rough card or foam maquettes) are appropriate early in development when the goal is to explore basic proportions and spatial relationships quickly and cheaply. High-fidelity prototypes, machined from production-equivalent materials and finished to near-production quality, are appropriate immediately before production sign-off, when the cost of an error is very high. Selecting the appropriate fidelity level is an important design judgement: over-investing in high fidelity too early wastes resources; using only low-fidelity models too late in the process risks missing important problems.

Common physical modelling materials include: card and cardboard (fast, cheap, appropriate for early architectural and product form studies); foam (polyurethane or polystyrene foam can be carved and sanded to complex organic forms, ideal for ergonomic study models); clay and modelling clay (Plastilene) for automotive and product form development; balsa wood and MDF for structural and architectural models; thermoplastics (ABS, acrylic) machined or formed for higher-fidelity models; and 3D printed components for rapid, accurate prototyping. Increasingly, digital fabrication tools (laser cutting, CNC routing, 3D printing) are used to produce physical models directly from CAD data.

Physical models offer advantages that digital and graphical representations cannot fully replicate. They can be held, manipulated, and experienced in three dimensions. They allow users to assess ergonomics, weight, balance, and haptic qualities. They are immediately intuitive to non-specialist stakeholders. They reveal spatial relationships and proportional issues that can be difficult to perceive on screen. However, physical models are more time-consuming and expensive to produce than digital models, and are less easily modified once constructed.

quiz.question: Distinguish between an appearance model, a working model, and a prototype in the context of physical modelling.
quiz.markscheme: An appearance model replicates the visual form, colour, and surface quality of a design but has no working parts — used for aesthetic evaluation and client presentations. A working model demonstrates specific functional aspects (mechanisms, interactions) but may not look like the final product. A prototype combines appearance and function, closely approximating the final product — used for user testing, engineering validation, and pre-production checks. The key distinctions are function, fidelity, and purpose.

quiz.question: Explain the concept of model fidelity and describe how a designer decides on the appropriate level of fidelity at different stages of product development.
quiz.markscheme: Fidelity refers to how closely a model replicates the final product. Early in development, low-fidelity models (rough foam or card) are appropriate because the goal is quick, cheap exploration of basic concepts — high fidelity would waste resources at this exploratory stage. Later in development, higher fidelity is required to test user experience, ergonomics, and engineering performance rigorously. Immediately before production, near-production-fidelity prototypes are needed to catch remaining issues. The choice is driven by what questions need to be answered and what the cost of an error at that stage would be.

quiz.question: Describe two materials used in physical model making and explain why each is suitable for the purpose described.
quiz.markscheme: Accept any two with clear justification: card/cardboard — cheap, fast to cut and fold, suitable for early form studies and spatial models; polyurethane foam — can be carved and sanded to complex forms, good for ergonomic and form development models; Plastilene/clay — malleable, supports organic forms, used in automotive styling; MDF/balsa — machinable to precise dimensions, suitable for structural or architectural models; ABS/acrylic — can be machined or thermoformed to high-fidelity appearance models. Each material should be matched to an appropriate model type or stage.

quiz.question: Analyse the advantages of physical models over digital CAD models when evaluating the ergonomics of a hand-held product.
quiz.markscheme: Physical models can be held and handled, allowing assessment of grip comfort, weight, balance, and hand-fit that cannot be replicated on screen. Users' physical interaction with a model reveals ergonomic issues — hotspots, awkward grip angles, excessive weight — that would not be apparent from a CAD visualisation. Physical models also allow iterative rapid adjustment through carving or adding material. Digital models are superior for precise dimensioning and sharing across teams, but are inferior for assessing the felt qualities of a product in use.

quiz.question: Discuss how digital fabrication technologies such as 3D printing and laser cutting have changed the role of physical modelling in product development.
quiz.markscheme: Digital fabrication enables physical models to be produced directly from CAD data, dramatically reducing the time and skill required for model construction. 3D printing allows complex geometries to be produced overnight without jigs, tools, or specialist craft skills. Laser cutting enables precise flat components to be produced quickly. This has made physical prototyping faster and cheaper, enabling more iterations in less time. However, not all materials and finishes are replicable by current digital fabrication, so skilled model-making remains valuable for high-fidelity appearance models. Overall, digital fabrication has made physical modelling more accessible and accelerated product development cycles.

title: Computer-aided design (CAD)
objectives: Define computer-aided design (CAD) and describe its main types.
Explain the difference between 2D CAD drafting and 3D parametric solid modelling.
Describe how CAD data is used downstream in engineering analysis and manufacturing.
Analyse the advantages of CAD over traditional manual design methods.
Discuss the limitations and challenges associated with CAD in industrial design practice.
summary:
Computer-aided design (CAD) refers to the use of computer software to create, modify, analyse, and optimise design representations. CAD has fundamentally transformed engineering and product design practice since its emergence in the 1960s and 1970s, displacing manual drafting as the standard method of producing technical documentation and becoming central to integrated digital product development workflows.

CAD exists in two primary modes. Two-dimensional (2D) CAD is the digital equivalent of manual drafting, producing precise drawings with dimensions, views, and annotations. Software such as AutoCAD remains widely used for 2D documentation, particularly in architecture and civil engineering. Three-dimensional (3D) CAD — particularly parametric solid modelling — is now the dominant mode in mechanical and product design. In parametric modelling, a 3D solid is defined by dimensions and geometric relationships ('parameters'); changing a parameter automatically updates the entire model and all associated drawings and analyses. This associativity greatly reduces the work of revisions. Leading 3D CAD packages include SolidWorks, CATIA, Inventor, Creo, and Fusion 360.

CAD data generated during design can be directly used in downstream engineering and manufacturing processes. Finite element analysis (FEA) applies simulated loads to a 3D CAD model to predict structural performance, identifying stress concentrations and potential failure points without building physical prototypes. Computational fluid dynamics (CFD) simulates fluid and airflow around or through a design. Computer-aided manufacturing (CAM) software uses CAD geometry to generate toolpaths for CNC machining, directly linking the design model to the machine that will produce the part. Rapid prototyping (3D printing) similarly takes CAD data directly as input, enabling physical models to be produced from the same file used for engineering analysis.

The advantages of CAD over manual drafting and traditional design methods are substantial. CAD enables much greater precision and consistency than manual drawing. Revisions are fast — changing a dimension in a parametric model propagates through all views and derived documents automatically. CAD files can be stored, versioned, retrieved, and transmitted electronically across global design and supply chain teams without loss of quality. 3D models enable visualisation, simulation, and virtual testing that would require expensive physical prototypes to assess otherwise. Assembly modelling allows interference checking — the computer identifies where two parts in an assembly would physically clash, catching errors before manufacture.

Limitations and challenges include: the high initial cost of professional CAD software licences and the hardware to run them; the significant training investment required to reach proficiency; the risk that designers focus on detailed 3D modelling too early, foreclosing creative exploration that is better done with sketches and physical models; and issues of file format compatibility between different CAD systems and between clients and suppliers. Intellectual property and data security are also concerns when sharing detailed CAD files across supply chains.

quiz.question: Distinguish between 2D CAD drafting and 3D parametric solid modelling.
quiz.markscheme: 2D CAD produces flat drawings with lines, dimensions, and annotations — the digital equivalent of manual drafting. It is used for orthographic views, floor plans, and schematic diagrams. 3D parametric solid modelling creates a three-dimensional solid body defined by dimensions and geometric relationships. Changing a parameter updates the model and all derived drawings automatically (associativity). 3D models support visualisation, simulation, and direct data transfer to CAM and rapid prototyping. 3D parametric modelling is now dominant in product and mechanical design.

quiz.question: Explain how finite element analysis (FEA) uses CAD data to evaluate a design's structural performance.
quiz.markscheme: FEA divides the CAD model geometry into thousands of small elements (the 'mesh'). Boundary conditions are applied — material properties, constraints (where the part is fixed), and loads (forces, pressures, temperatures). The software calculates stress, strain, and deflection at each element. Results are visualised as colour maps showing stress distributions. This allows designers to identify stress concentrations and potential failure points without building physical prototypes, reducing cost and accelerating development. Material and geometry can be iteratively optimised based on FEA results.

quiz.question: Describe how CAD data is used to generate toolpaths for CNC machining (CAM), and explain the advantage of this integration.
quiz.markscheme: CAM software takes the 3D CAD geometry as input and generates machine-readable toolpaths that define how a cutting tool moves relative to the workpiece to produce the required shape. The advantage of direct CAD-to-CAM data transfer is that dimensional information is carried directly from the design file to the machine — eliminating transcription errors from manual measurement or re-drawing. Changes to the CAD model can be quickly reflected in updated toolpaths. This integration is a key enabler of rapid, accurate, and consistent computer-integrated manufacturing.

quiz.question: Analyse three specific advantages of parametric 3D CAD over manual drafting methods.
quiz.markscheme: Accept any three: automatic update of all views when a dimension is changed (parametric associativity reduces revision time); interference checking in assemblies (identifies component clashes before manufacture); integration with simulation tools (FEA, CFD) without re-entering geometry; direct input to CAM and rapid prototyping; electronic storage, versioning, and global sharing of design data; photorealistic visualisation for client presentations; ability to generate exploded assembly views and BOMs (bills of materials) automatically. Each advantage should be briefly explained.

quiz.question: Discuss one limitation of CAD use in product design practice and suggest how it might be managed.
quiz.markscheme: Accept one with discussion: high cost of professional licences (managed by subscription models, open-source alternatives like FreeCAD, or cloud-based tools like Fusion 360 for SMEs); significant training investment (managed by structured training programmes, online resources, and building proficiency incrementally); risk of premature detail commitment — 3D modelling can discourage early exploration (managed by explicitly using sketching and physical modelling in early stages before CAD); file format incompatibility across supply chains (managed by industry-standard neutral formats such as STEP and IGES, and by clear supplier data requirements). The answer should name a specific limitation and a credible management strategy.

title: Rapid prototyping
objectives: Define rapid prototyping and explain how it differs from traditional prototyping methods.
Describe the main additive manufacturing technologies used in rapid prototyping.
Explain the process of preparing a CAD model for rapid prototyping using STL file format.
Analyse the advantages and limitations of rapid prototyping in product development.
Discuss applications of rapid prototyping beyond product design, such as medical and aerospace use.
summary:
Rapid prototyping (RP) refers to a group of technologies that produce physical objects directly and automatically from digital CAD data, without the need for traditional tooling, moulds, or machining set-up. The term 'rapid' reflects the speed advantage over traditional model-making: a complex form that might take a skilled model-maker several days to produce by hand can often be printed overnight. The dominant rapid prototyping technologies are additive manufacturing processes — they build objects layer by layer by adding material, rather than removing it from a solid block.

The main additive manufacturing technologies are: Fused Deposition Modelling (FDM), also called Fused Filament Fabrication (FFF), which extrudes melted thermoplastic (commonly PLA or ABS) through a nozzle in thin layers — the most widely available and affordable technology, used in desktop 3D printers; Stereolithography (SLA), which uses an ultraviolet laser to cure liquid photopolymer resin layer by layer — produces high-resolution, smooth-surface parts suitable for detailed appearance models; Selective Laser Sintering (SLS), which uses a laser to fuse powdered nylon or metal powders — produces functional, strong parts without support structures and is widely used for functional prototypes; and Direct Metal Laser Sintering (DMLS) or Selective Laser Melting (SLM), which sinter or melt metal powders to produce complex metal parts used in aerospace, medical, and high-performance engineering applications.

Preparing a CAD model for rapid prototyping involves converting the 3D solid model into the STL (Standard Tessellation Language) file format, which approximates the surface of the model as a mesh of small triangles. The STL file is then processed by slicing software, which divides the model into thin horizontal layers and generates the machine instructions (toolpaths or laser paths) for each layer. Slicing software also adds support structures where overhanging geometry requires them, and allows control of layer thickness (resolution), infill density, and other process parameters.

Advantages of rapid prototyping include: speed (hours to days rather than days to weeks); no tooling cost (each part costs the same regardless of whether one or ten are made); ability to produce complex geometries that would be impossible or prohibitively expensive to machine; direct integration with CAD data; and enabling rapid design iteration. Limitations include: surface finish inferior to injection-moulded parts (though SLA parts can achieve good surface quality); limited material range compared to conventional manufacturing; parts produced by FDM may have anisotropic mechanical properties (weaker in the build direction); build volume limits the size of parts; and cost per part is typically higher than injection moulding at volume.

Rapid prototyping applications extend well beyond product development. In medicine, patient-specific anatomical models are printed from MRI and CT scan data to support surgical planning; custom prosthetics and orthotics are produced to individual patient geometry; and increasingly, bioprinting is exploring the printing of biological tissues. In aerospace, DMLS-produced metal components with complex internal cooling channels — impossible to machine conventionally — are used in turbine blades and rocket engines. Dental crowns and orthopaedic implants are manufactured by DMLS to tight tolerances and individual geometries.

quiz.question: Define rapid prototyping and explain what distinguishes it from traditional subtractive model-making.
quiz.markscheme: Rapid prototyping (RP) refers to additive manufacturing technologies that build physical objects layer by layer directly from CAD data, without tooling or machining set-up. Traditional subtractive model-making removes material from a solid block by cutting, carving, or machining — requiring jigs, tools, and skilled craftsperson time. RP is faster (hours to days), requires no dedicated tooling, can produce complex internal geometries, and translates directly from digital data. Subtractive methods can achieve superior surface finishes and material properties in some applications.

quiz.question: Describe the fused deposition modelling (FDM) process and identify one advantage and one limitation of this technology.
quiz.markscheme: FDM extrudes melted thermoplastic filament (commonly PLA or ABS) through a heated nozzle, depositing it in thin layers on a build platform. Each layer bonds to the previous one as it cools, building up the part layer by layer. Advantage: widely available and affordable; desktop machines are accessible to design studios and schools. Limitation: layer lines are visible, giving a rougher surface finish than SLA; parts can be mechanically weaker in the build (Z) direction due to inter-layer bonding (anisotropy).

quiz.question: Explain the role of the STL file format in preparing a CAD model for 3D printing.
quiz.markscheme: STL (Standard Tessellation Language) approximates the surface of a 3D CAD solid as a mesh of triangles. It is the most widely used format for transferring geometry to rapid prototyping machines. The STL file is input to slicing software, which divides the geometry into thin horizontal layers and generates machine instructions for each layer. The triangle mesh resolution determines the accuracy of curved surface representation — a coarser mesh produces faceted surfaces; a finer mesh more closely approximates the original CAD geometry but produces larger file sizes.

quiz.question: Analyse three advantages of rapid prototyping that make it valuable in product development.
quiz.markscheme: Accept any three with explanation: speed — complex parts produced in hours rather than days, enabling faster design iteration; no tooling cost — allows one-off or small-batch parts to be produced economically; complex geometry — internal channels, lattices, and organic forms impossible or expensive to machine conventionally can be printed; direct CAD integration — no transcription or tooling programming required; design iteration — multiple variants can be printed simultaneously for comparative testing. Each advantage should be explained in context.

quiz.question: Discuss one application of rapid prototyping in the medical field and explain why additive manufacturing is particularly suited to this application.
quiz.markscheme: Relevant examples: patient-specific surgical planning models printed from CT/MRI scan data allow surgeons to study anatomy before complex operations, reducing surgical risk and time (additive manufacturing is suited because each patient has a unique geometry — no other process can produce individual one-off parts cost-effectively); custom prosthetics and orthotics fitted exactly to an individual patient (suited because patient-specific geometry is data-driven and unique); DMLS-produced orthopaedic implants with porous lattice surfaces that promote bone in-growth. The answer should explain why the patient-specific or geometrically complex nature of the application suits additive manufacturing specifically.

title: Properties of materials
objectives: Define and distinguish between mechanical properties of materials including strength, hardness, toughness, and elasticity.
Explain the difference between tensile, compressive, and shear stresses.
Describe key physical and thermal properties relevant to material selection.
Apply material property knowledge to justify material selection for a given design context.
Explain how material testing is used to determine mechanical properties.
summary:
Material selection is one of the most consequential decisions a product designer makes. The suitability of a material for a given application depends on matching its properties to the demands of the product — the loads it will carry, the environment it will operate in, the manufacturing processes available, and the cost, weight, and aesthetic requirements. A thorough understanding of material properties is therefore fundamental design knowledge.

Mechanical properties describe how a material responds to applied forces. Strength is the ability to resist applied forces without permanent deformation or fracture; it is divided into tensile strength (resistance to being pulled apart), compressive strength (resistance to being squashed), and shear strength (resistance to parallel opposing forces). Hardness is the resistance to surface indentation or scratching — important for wear resistance in bearings, tools, and floors. Toughness is the ability to absorb energy and resist fracture — a tough material can deform plastically before fracturing, absorbing impact energy (like a crash helmet absorber). Elasticity is the ability to deform under load and return to the original shape when the load is removed (the opposite of plasticity); stiffness (modulus of elasticity or Young's modulus) describes resistance to elastic deformation.

Ductility is the ability to be drawn into wire or permanently deformed without fracturing — important for forming and drawing operations. Malleability is the ability to be hammered or pressed into shape — similar to ductility but in compression. Brittleness is the tendency to fracture without significant plastic deformation — glass and cast iron are brittle materials; they are strong in compression but fail suddenly under tension or impact without warning.

Physical properties include density (mass per unit volume — critical for weight-sensitive applications), electrical conductivity (for electrical and electronic applications), thermal conductivity (for heat sinks, cookware, and insulation), optical properties (transparency, reflectivity), and magnetic properties. Thermal properties include the coefficient of thermal expansion (how much a material expands when heated — important for joints and seals between dissimilar materials), melting point (critical for processing and high-temperature applications), and specific heat capacity.

Material testing methods are used to determine these properties quantitatively. The tensile test stretches a standardised specimen to failure, producing a stress–strain curve from which Young's modulus, yield strength, ultimate tensile strength (UTS), and ductility can all be read. The Vickers, Brinell, and Rockwell hardness tests measure resistance to indentation under a standardised load. The Charpy and Izod impact tests measure toughness by striking a notched specimen with a pendulum. Fatigue testing subjects a specimen to repeated cyclic loading to determine the endurance limit.

In IB Design & Technology, students are expected to select materials for specific applications by referencing and comparing properties, justifying their choice against the design context, and recognising that material selection involves trade-offs — no material is optimal in all properties simultaneously.

quiz.question: Define hardness and toughness, and explain why a material that is very hard is not necessarily very tough.
quiz.markscheme: Hardness is the resistance to surface indentation or scratching. Toughness is the ability to absorb energy and resist fracture — it requires some capacity for plastic deformation before fracture. Very hard materials (e.g. tool steel, glass) are typically brittle — they resist scratching but fracture suddenly without significant plastic deformation when impacted. Toughness requires a balance of strength and ductility. Glass is hard but brittle (not tough); rubber is tough but not hard. Ceramics are very hard but very brittle.

quiz.question: Distinguish between tensile stress, compressive stress, and shear stress, and give one design application where each is the dominant loading type.
quiz.markscheme: Tensile stress: a material is pulled apart by forces acting in opposite directions along the same axis — e.g. a climbing rope or bridge cable. Compressive stress: forces are applied towards each other along the same axis, squashing the material — e.g. the legs of a table or columns in a building. Shear stress: opposing parallel forces act across the material — e.g. a bolt in a bolted joint, or the blade of scissors cutting material.

quiz.question: Explain what a stress–strain curve reveals about the mechanical properties of a material, identifying at least three properties that can be read from it.
quiz.markscheme: A stress–strain curve plots stress (force per unit area) against strain (proportional deformation) for a material under tensile test. Properties readable from the curve: Young's modulus (slope of the elastic region = stiffness); yield strength (stress at which plastic deformation begins — the elastic limit); ultimate tensile strength (highest stress on the curve — maximum load-bearing capacity); ductility/elongation at fracture (total strain at failure — measure of how much the material deforms before fracture). The curve shows whether the material is elastic, plastic, brittle, or ductile.

quiz.question: Apply your knowledge of material properties to justify the selection of aluminium alloy for the frame of a racing bicycle.
quiz.markscheme: Aluminium alloy has a high strength-to-weight ratio (high tensile strength with low density — critical for a lightweight racing frame); good stiffness (efficient power transfer); corrosion resistance (no surface treatment needed for most applications); relatively easy to form and weld into complex tube profiles; cost-effective compared to carbon fibre. Limitations: less stiff than carbon fibre (so some compliance/flex); not as easily repaired as steel. The answer should match specific properties to the specific requirements of a racing bicycle frame.

quiz.question: Describe the Charpy impact test and explain what material property it is used to measure.
quiz.markscheme: The Charpy test strikes a notched specimen with a swinging pendulum of known energy. The energy absorbed by the specimen as it fractures is measured by the height the pendulum swings to after impact (compared to its release height). This measures toughness (impact energy absorption). A material with high Charpy impact energy is tough; one with low energy absorption is brittle. The notch concentrates stress, simulating real-world stress concentrations. The test is widely used to evaluate materials for structural applications subject to impact loading.

title: Metals and metallic alloys
objectives: Distinguish between ferrous and non-ferrous metals and give examples of each.
Describe the properties and typical applications of common metals including steel, aluminium, copper, and titanium.
Explain how alloying changes the properties of a base metal.
Describe the effects of heat treatment on the properties of steel.
Analyse the factors that influence the selection of a metal or alloy for a product design application.
summary:
Metals are crystalline inorganic materials characterised by high electrical and thermal conductivity, metallic lustre, and generally high strength and ductility. They are the most widely used class of engineering material in product design, construction, and manufacturing. Metals are classified broadly as ferrous (containing iron as the primary constituent) or non-ferrous (no significant iron content).

Ferrous metals include cast iron and the various grades of steel. Steel is an alloy of iron and carbon (typically 0.1–1.5% carbon), with other alloying elements added to impart specific properties. Low-carbon (mild) steel is cheap, easily formed and welded, and widely used for structural sections, sheet metal parts, and car body panels. Medium-carbon steel is stronger and harder but less ductile. High-carbon steel is very hard and used for tools and springs. Stainless steel (alloyed with chromium and nickel) is highly corrosion resistant and is used for cutlery, medical instruments, and kitchen equipment. Cast iron has high compressive strength but is brittle; it is used for engine blocks and heavy machine bases.

Non-ferrous metals include aluminium, copper, zinc, tin, lead, nickel, and titanium. Aluminium is low density, corrosion resistant (forms a protective oxide layer), and has good electrical and thermal conductivity; its alloys achieve high strength-to-weight ratios, making it the material of choice for aerospace, transportation, and packaging. Copper has very high electrical and thermal conductivity, corrosion resistance, and is easily worked; it is used for electrical wiring, plumbing, and heat exchangers. Brass (copper–zinc alloy) and bronze (copper–tin alloy) have specific properties suited to valves, bearings, and musical instruments. Titanium has an exceptional strength-to-weight ratio, high corrosion resistance, and biocompatibility; it is used in aerospace components, medical implants, and sports equipment, though at significantly higher cost than aluminium or steel.

Alloying is the process of combining a base metal with one or more additional elements to improve or modify its properties. Carbon in steel increases hardness and strength (at the expense of ductility). Chromium in steel improves corrosion resistance. Nickel in steel improves toughness and corrosion resistance. Silicon in aluminium improves castability. Alloys are therefore engineered materials — their composition is chosen to deliver a specific combination of properties that no pure metal provides.

Heat treatment processes alter the microstructure of metals and thereby change their mechanical properties. Annealing involves heating steel above a critical temperature and cooling slowly, which softens the metal and improves ductility and machinability. Quenching (hardening) involves heating and then cooling rapidly in water or oil, trapping a hard, brittle microstructure (martensite). Tempering follows quenching, reheating to a lower temperature to reduce brittleness while retaining significant hardness. Case hardening produces a hard, wear-resistant surface layer while maintaining a tough core.

Metal selection for a specific product involves weighing multiple factors: required mechanical properties (strength, hardness, toughness, fatigue resistance), physical properties (density, conductivity), corrosion environment, manufacturing process compatibility, cost, availability, and end-of-life recyclability. Most metals are readily recyclable with minimal loss of properties, making them attractive from a sustainability perspective — aluminium recycling, for example, uses only 5% of the energy of primary production.

quiz.question: Distinguish between ferrous and non-ferrous metals and give two examples of each with typical applications.
quiz.markscheme: Ferrous metals contain iron as the primary constituent: e.g. mild steel (car body panels, structural sections), stainless steel (cutlery, medical instruments). Non-ferrous metals contain no significant iron: e.g. aluminium (aerospace frames, drink cans), copper (electrical wiring, plumbing). Ferrous metals are generally magnetic and prone to corrosion unless treated or alloyed; non-ferrous metals are generally non-magnetic and often more corrosion resistant.

quiz.question: Explain how increasing the carbon content of steel affects its properties.
quiz.markscheme: Increasing carbon content increases hardness and tensile strength but reduces ductility and weldability. Low-carbon (mild) steel (~0.1–0.3% C) is soft, ductile, and easily welded — suitable for general structural use. Medium-carbon steel (0.3–0.6% C) is stronger and harder — suitable for machinery parts and rails. High-carbon steel (0.6–1.5% C) is very hard and strong but brittle — suitable for cutting tools, springs, and high-wear components. Beyond ~0.8% C, steel becomes increasingly difficult to weld.

quiz.question: Describe the quench hardening and tempering heat treatment process for steel, explaining the purpose of each stage.
quiz.markscheme: Quench hardening: steel is heated above the critical temperature (austenitising temperature, ~850°C for medium-carbon steel) and then rapidly cooled (quenched) in water or oil. Rapid cooling traps the iron in a hard, metastable crystal structure called martensite, making the steel very hard but also brittle. Tempering: the quenched steel is reheated to a lower temperature (150–650°C depending on required properties) and held before cooling. This allows some reorganisation of the microstructure, reducing brittleness while retaining significant hardness. The higher the tempering temperature, the tougher (and less hard) the resulting steel.

quiz.question: Analyse why aluminium alloys are preferred over steel for the structural frames of commercial aircraft.
quiz.markscheme: Aluminium alloys have a much lower density than steel (~2.7 vs ~7.8 g/cm³) but their high-strength grades achieve comparable specific strength (strength per unit weight). This gives a significant weight saving, directly reducing fuel consumption and operating cost — critical in aviation. Aluminium also has good corrosion resistance (important in humid atmospheric environments), can be formed into complex extruded and sheet profiles, and is readily joinable. Limitations include lower stiffness per unit weight than carbon fibre (which is increasingly used in modern aircraft) and work hardening that limits forming operations.

quiz.question: Discuss the sustainability considerations relevant to metal selection in product design.
quiz.markscheme: Most metals are highly recyclable with low property degradation — steel and aluminium both have well-established recycling streams. Aluminium recycling uses only ~5% of the energy of primary production, giving large lifecycle energy savings. However, primary metal production has significant environmental impacts: mining, smelting, and refining consume large amounts of energy and generate pollution. Rare or geopolitically concentrated metals (e.g. cobalt, rare earths) raise supply security concerns. Designers should consider recyclability, recycled content, regional availability, and extraction impact when selecting metals. Designing for disassembly facilitates metal recovery at end of life.

title: Timber
objectives: Distinguish between hardwood and softwood timber, explaining the botanical basis of the distinction.
Describe the key properties of timber that affect its use in product design.
Explain how the grain structure and moisture content of timber affect its performance.
Describe common timber-based manufactured boards and their applications.
Discuss the sustainability considerations relevant to the use of timber in product design.
summary:
Timber is one of the oldest and most widely used design materials, valued for its natural aesthetic, workability, structural capability, and, when responsibly sourced, its renewable character. In IB Design & Technology, timber is studied as both a natural material (solid wood) and as a processed form (manufactured boards), and students must understand the properties, applications, and sustainability implications of each.

The classification of timber as hardwood or softwood is botanical rather than purely descriptive of hardness. Hardwoods come from deciduous (broad-leaved) trees that lose their leaves annually — examples include oak, ash, teak, mahogany, beech, and balsa (which, despite being a hardwood botanically, is very soft physically). Softwoods come from coniferous (cone-bearing, needle-leaved, mostly evergreen) trees such as pine, spruce, fir, and cedar. In general, hardwoods tend to be denser, harder, and more durable than softwoods, though there is considerable overlap; they also tend to grow more slowly, making them more expensive. Softwoods grow faster, are more abundant, less expensive, and are widely used in construction and furniture framing.

Timber properties important to design include strength (varies with species and grain direction — timber is strongest parallel to the grain), hardness (surface resistance to indentation), durability (resistance to decay and insect attack without treatment — naturally durable species like teak contain oils that resist moisture), flexibility, and workability (how easily timber can be cut, joined, and finished). Timber is anisotropic: its properties differ significantly along and across the grain, and across the three principal directions (longitudinal, radial, tangential).

Moisture content significantly affects timber's dimensional stability. Wood absorbs and releases moisture in response to the ambient humidity, causing it to expand (swell) across the grain and contract (shrink) as moisture content changes. This is why solid wood furniture joints are designed with floating panels to allow seasonal movement, and why timber must be seasoned (dried to a moisture content appropriate to its end-use environment) before use. Kiln-drying is the industrial method of seasoning; air-drying is slower but requires no energy input.

Manufactured boards are engineered wood products made by bonding wood fibres, veneers, or chips together with adhesive under heat and pressure. They include plywood (layers of veneer bonded at 90° to each other — good dimensional stability and strength in all directions), MDF (medium-density fibreboard — fine wood fibres bonded with urea-formaldehyde resin — smooth surface, easily machined and painted, but heavy and not moisture-resistant), chipboard/particleboard (wood chips bonded with adhesive — cheap, widely used in flat-pack furniture), and OSB (oriented strand board — coarse strands arranged in layers — structural use in construction). Manufactured boards use otherwise waste wood and can have more consistent properties than solid timber.

The sustainability of timber depends critically on how it is sourced. Certified sustainable forestry schemes (FSC — Forest Stewardship Council; PEFC — Programme for the Endorsement of Forest Certification) verify that timber comes from responsibly managed forests where harvesting rates do not exceed regrowth rates. Unsustainable logging, particularly of tropical hardwoods, contributes to deforestation, biodiversity loss, and carbon release. Designers should specify certified timber, favour local species over imported tropical hardwoods where possible, and consider the use of reclaimed or recycled timber.

quiz.question: Distinguish between hardwood and softwood, explaining both the botanical basis of the distinction and giving one example of each with an application.
quiz.markscheme: Hardwoods come from deciduous (broad-leaved) trees — e.g. oak (furniture, flooring). Softwoods come from coniferous (needle-leaved) trees — e.g. pine (construction framing, joinery). The distinction is botanical, not physical — balsa is a botanically hardwood but physically very soft. In general, hardwoods tend to be denser and more durable, and softwoods grow faster and are more widely available and cheaper. Students should note the botanical basis, not simply state 'hardwoods are harder.'

quiz.question: Explain why timber is described as anisotropic and describe two design implications of this characteristic.
quiz.markscheme: Timber is anisotropic because its mechanical properties (strength, stiffness, splitting) differ depending on the direction relative to the grain. Parallel to the grain: high tensile and compressive strength. Perpendicular to the grain: lower strength; timber splits easily across the grain. Design implications: structural members should be oriented so the grain is parallel to the primary load direction; joints must be designed to avoid stresses that would split along the grain; and allowance must be made for differential expansion and contraction across the grain due to moisture changes.

quiz.question: Describe how moisture content affects solid timber and explain why timber must be seasoned before use in furniture.
quiz.markscheme: Timber absorbs and releases moisture with changes in humidity, causing dimensional changes (swelling and shrinking) primarily across the grain. Unseasoned (green) timber has high moisture content and will shrink, warp, or crack as it dries in service, causing joints to fail and surfaces to split. Seasoning (either air-drying or kiln-drying) reduces moisture content to a level appropriate to the end-use environment, stabilising the material. Kiln-drying is faster and more controllable; air-drying is energy-free but slower.

quiz.question: Compare the properties and applications of plywood and MDF as manufactured boards.
quiz.markscheme: Plywood: alternating layers of veneer bonded at 90° — strong and stiff in all directions in-plane, good impact resistance, can be made in large sheets, suitable for structural and marine use (exterior-grade plywood), less smooth surface. MDF (medium-density fibreboard): fine wood fibres bonded with resin — very smooth, uniform surface excellent for painting and veneering, easily machined to precise profiles, consistent properties (no grain or knots), but heavy, absorbs moisture (not suitable for exterior/wet environments), and weaker/less stiff than plywood. MDF is widely used for flat-pack furniture carcasses and interior joinery; plywood for structural panels, marine applications, and formwork.

quiz.question: Discuss the role of FSC certification in ensuring timber is used sustainably in product design.
quiz.markscheme: FSC (Forest Stewardship Council) is an independent certification scheme that verifies timber comes from forests managed according to social, environmental, and economic sustainability criteria — harvesting does not exceed regrowth, biodiversity is maintained, and workers are treated fairly. Specifying FSC-certified timber ensures a designer's product does not contribute to deforestation. Limitations: certification is not universal — some producers in high-biodiversity tropical regions are not certified; there have been criticism of enforcement rigour in some regions. However, FSC remains the most widely recognised and credible independent timber certification system available to designers.

title: Glass
objectives: Describe the composition and structure of glass and explain why it is classified as an amorphous material.
Explain the key properties of glass that are relevant to product design.
Describe how toughened (tempered) and laminated glass are produced and explain their enhanced safety properties.
Analyse the applications of different glass types in product design contexts.
Discuss the environmental considerations relevant to the use of glass.
summary:
Glass is a hard, brittle, transparent material formed by melting silica (silicon dioxide, SiO₂) together with other oxide compounds (such as sodium oxide, calcium oxide, and boron trioxide) and cooling the melt rapidly enough to prevent crystallisation. Unlike crystalline solids, glass has a random, disordered atomic structure — it is described as an amorphous solid or supercooled liquid. This disordered structure gives glass its transparency but also contributes to its brittleness, because there are no crystal grain boundaries to arrest crack propagation; cracks in glass spread rapidly.

Soda-lime glass (also called soda-lime-silica glass) is the most common type, accounting for approximately 90% of glass production. It is used for windows, containers, and tableware. It is made from silica (SiO₂), soda ash (Na₂O), and lime (CaO). Borosilicate glass (known commercially as Pyrex) replaces some of the soda and lime with boron trioxide, giving it a much lower coefficient of thermal expansion and high resistance to thermal shock — it can withstand rapid temperature changes without cracking, making it suitable for laboratory glassware, baking dishes, and high-intensity lamps. Lead crystal glass incorporates lead oxide, increasing refractive index and giving it a brilliant sparkle — used for decorative glassware.

The key engineering properties of glass include: very high compressive strength (glass performs well under compression); low tensile strength (brittle fracture occurs at relatively low tensile stress due to surface microcracks); hardness (resistance to scratching — Mohs hardness ~6–7); chemical inertness (glass does not react with most chemicals, making it ideal for food, chemical, and medical containers); transparency (optical clarity across the visible spectrum); and thermal insulation (though conductivity is higher than polymer foams, double and triple glazing uses trapped air or gas to improve insulation).

Toughened (tempered) glass is produced by heating glass to near its softening point and then rapidly quenching the surfaces. This puts the surface layers into compression and the core into tension. Because glass fails in tension, surface compressive stresses must be overcome before tensile failure can initiate, making toughened glass approximately four to five times stronger than annealed glass in bending and impact. When toughened glass does fail, it shatters into small, blunt-edged fragments (dicing) rather than sharp shards, significantly reducing injury risk. Toughened glass is used in vehicle side windows, shower screens, furniture, and smartphone screens. Laminated glass consists of two or more glass panes bonded together with a polyvinyl butyral (PVB) interlayer. When broken, fragments are held in place by the interlayer, preventing shattering. Laminated glass is used in windscreens, architectural glazing, and security applications.

Glass is 100% recyclable without loss of quality — cullet (crushed glass) can be added to the glass batch to reduce the melting temperature and energy consumption, giving glass a strong environmental recycling case. However, glass is heavy (density ~2.5 g/cm³) compared to plastics, which increases transport energy. Mixed-colour glass is less easily recycled than colour-separated glass, so colour sorting at collection is important.

quiz.question: Explain why glass is classified as an amorphous material and describe one consequence of this structure for its mechanical behaviour.
quiz.markscheme: Glass has a random, disordered atomic structure (no regular crystal lattice) — it is an amorphous solid formed by cooling a melt too rapidly for crystallisation to occur. Consequence: because there are no grain boundaries to arrest crack propagation (as in crystalline metals), cracks in glass spread rapidly and catastrophically once initiated, making glass brittle. Glass fractures suddenly without significant plastic deformation, unlike ductile metals.

quiz.question: Compare the properties of soda-lime glass and borosilicate glass, and explain why borosilicate glass is used for laboratory equipment.
quiz.markscheme: Soda-lime glass: most common, inexpensive, good optical clarity, suitable for windows and containers, but has a relatively high coefficient of thermal expansion — susceptible to cracking under thermal shock. Borosilicate glass: contains boron trioxide which significantly reduces the coefficient of thermal expansion, giving very high resistance to thermal shock (can withstand rapid temperature changes). It is used for laboratory glassware because it can be heated and cooled rapidly without cracking, and it is chemically inert and resists most laboratory reagents.

quiz.question: Describe the process of producing toughened (tempered) glass and explain why it is safer than annealed glass when it breaks.
quiz.markscheme: Toughened glass is produced by heating annealed glass to near its softening point (~600°C) and then rapidly quenching the surfaces with cold air. This places the surface layers in compression and the interior in tension. The compressive stresses must be overcome before tensile failure can initiate, making toughened glass 4–5× stronger than annealed glass. When it does fail, it shatters into small, roughly cubic, blunt-edged fragments (dicing) rather than sharp shards, greatly reducing injury risk. This is why it is used in vehicle side windows and shower screens.

quiz.question: Explain how laminated glass differs from toughened glass and identify one application where laminated glass is preferred.
quiz.markscheme: Laminated glass bonds two or more glass panes with a PVB (polyvinyl butyral) interlayer under heat and pressure. When broken, the fragments are held in place by the interlayer — the glass 'crazes' but does not shatter and fall. This is different from toughened glass, which shatters into small dice. Laminated glass is preferred for windscreens (automotive) because it maintains a clear viewing area after impact (the crazing pattern is visible but the screen holds together), and for security/architectural glazing where fragments must be retained.

quiz.question: Discuss the environmental advantages and limitations of glass as a packaging material compared to plastics.
quiz.markscheme: Advantages: glass is 100% recyclable without loss of quality; cullet reduces melting energy; chemically inert (no leaching concerns); long service life; visible recycling infrastructure and high consumer recycling rates in many countries. Limitations: glass is heavy (~2.5 g/cm³ vs ~1 g/cm³ for PET), increasing transport energy and carbon footprint per unit volume; brittle — breakage in transport creates waste; higher energy requirement for primary production than many plastics. Overall: glass has a strong recycling case but a heavier lifecycle transport footprint; the comparison depends on the specific application and the recycling rates and infrastructure available.

title: Plastics
objectives: Distinguish between thermoplastics and thermosetting plastics in terms of structure and behaviour.
Describe the properties and common applications of key thermoplastics including PE, PP, PET, ABS, and acrylic.
Describe the properties and applications of key thermosets including epoxy, polyester resin, and urea-formaldehyde.
Explain how the molecular structure of plastics determines their thermal behaviour.
Discuss the environmental challenges associated with plastics and strategies to address them.
summary:
Plastics (polymers) are a vast class of synthetic and semi-synthetic organic materials that have transformed product design over the twentieth century. Their combination of low density, chemical resistance, ease of moulding to complex shapes, electrical insulation, and low cost has made them ubiquitous in consumer goods, packaging, electronics, automotive components, and medical devices. Understanding the fundamental difference between thermoplastics and thermosetting plastics is essential, as it determines processability, recyclability, and end-of-life options.

Thermoplastics have a linear or branched molecular chain structure with no (or weak) cross-links between chains. They soften and flow when heated (above their glass transition temperature Tg or melting point) and re-solidify on cooling. This behaviour is reversible, which means thermoplastics can be repeatedly melted and remoulded — and in principle recycled. Common thermoplastics include: polyethylene (PE — used for bags, bottles, pipes, in low-density LDPE and high-density HDPE grades); polypropylene (PP — packaging, automotive components, medical devices — good chemical resistance and living hinge capability); polyethylene terephthalate (PET — drinks bottles, food packaging, fibres); acrylonitrile-butadiene-styrene (ABS — LEGO bricks, consumer electronics casings, automotive trim — good impact resistance and surface quality); and polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA, acrylic/Perspex — transparent panels, signs, lenses — high optical clarity and weather resistance).

Thermosetting plastics (thermosets) undergo an irreversible chemical cross-linking reaction during curing, forming a three-dimensional network of covalent bonds. Once cured, they cannot be re-melted — heating a thermoset will degrade it rather than liquefy it. This makes thermosets impossible to recycle by conventional melt-reprocessing, but they offer high heat resistance, dimensional stability, and rigidity that thermoplastics cannot achieve at elevated temperatures. Key thermosets include: epoxy resin (used as adhesives and as the matrix in carbon fibre composites — excellent mechanical properties and adhesion); polyester resin (used as matrix in glass fibre composites — cheaper than epoxy but lower performance); urea-formaldehyde (UF) resin (used as adhesive in MDF and chipboard, and for electrical fittings — hard and rigid but releases formaldehyde, raising health concerns); and phenol-formaldehyde (Bakelite — one of the earliest synthetic plastics, used for electrical fittings and knobs).

The thermal behaviour difference between thermoplastics and thermosets is explained by molecular structure. Thermoplastics have chain-to-chain intermolecular forces (van der Waals, dipole interactions, hydrogen bonds) that weaken on heating, allowing chains to flow past each other. Thermosets have covalent cross-links between chains that do not break on heating — the network remains rigid until thermal degradation destroys the chains themselves.

Plastics present significant environmental challenges. Most are derived from fossil fuels (petrochemicals), contributing to carbon emissions in production. Many have very long environmental lifespans — resisting biological degradation for centuries — leading to accumulation in landfill and oceans. Microplastics, resulting from the fragmentation of plastic waste, are now found throughout marine ecosystems and food chains. Strategies to address plastic environmental impact include: mechanical recycling (sorting and remelting thermoplastics); chemical recycling (breaking polymers back to monomers); development of bio-based and biodegradable polymers (PLA — polylactic acid — from starch); reducing single-use plastics through regulation and design; and designing products for mono-material construction to simplify recycling.

quiz.question: Explain the difference between thermoplastics and thermosetting plastics in terms of molecular structure and thermal behaviour.
quiz.markscheme: Thermoplastics have linear or branched chains held together by weak intermolecular forces. On heating, these forces weaken and chains can flow — the material softens and can be remoulded. This is reversible. Thermosets have covalent cross-links forming a 3D network. These cross-links do not soften on heating; the material remains rigid until the temperature is high enough to degrade the polymer chains. This is irreversible. Thermoplastics can (in principle) be recycled by remelting; thermosets cannot.

quiz.question: Describe the properties of ABS and explain why it is widely used for consumer electronics casings.
quiz.markscheme: ABS (acrylonitrile-butadiene-styrene) is a terpolymer combining the rigidity and chemical resistance of acrylonitrile, the toughness and impact resistance of butadiene rubber, and the processability and surface quality of styrene. For electronics casings, it offers: good impact resistance (survives drops without shattering); good dimensional stability; excellent surface quality for painting and texturing; easy processing by injection moulding to complex geometries; and relatively low cost. It also accepts adhesives and surface treatments well. Limitation: not suitable for high temperatures or outdoor UV exposure (yellows and becomes brittle).

quiz.question: Explain why epoxy resin is classified as a thermosetting plastic and describe one application in which its thermosetting properties are advantageous.
quiz.markscheme: Epoxy undergoes an irreversible cross-linking reaction when mixed with a hardener, forming a rigid three-dimensional covalent network. It cannot be re-melted — it is a thermoset. This is advantageous in composite manufacture (e.g. carbon fibre reinforced polymer — CFRP): the cured epoxy matrix holds carbon fibres in their precise geometry, provides load transfer between fibres, and resists the temperatures and loads encountered in aerospace components. The dimensional stability and resistance to creep at elevated temperatures of thermosets are essential in these applications — a thermoplastic matrix would soften and deform.

quiz.question: Analyse why plastics with mixed polymer types are difficult to recycle mechanically.
quiz.markscheme: Mechanical recycling of plastics requires polymer sorting because different polymers have different melt temperatures, rheologies, and properties — if mixed, the resulting recyclate has poor, unpredictable properties (e.g. PE and PET are incompatible). Multi-layer packaging and mixed-plastic products cannot be separated cost-effectively, so they are either downcycled (converted to lower-quality applications) or sent to landfill. This is why designing products from mono-materials or using clearly labelled, separable material combinations is important for recyclability. Identification symbols (recycling codes 1–7) assist sorting but manual and automated sorting remains imperfect.

quiz.question: Discuss two strategies designers can use to reduce the environmental impact of plastics in consumer products.
quiz.markscheme: Accept any two with discussion: use of bio-based plastics (e.g. PLA from starch — reduces fossil fuel dependence, may be compostable under industrial conditions; limitations: food vs fuel competition, may still not biodegrade in marine environments); designing for mono-material construction (single polymer type throughout — simplifies mechanical recycling, increases recyclate quality); eliminating unnecessary plastic (reducing material quantity overall, questioning whether plastic is needed); using recycled content (reducing demand for virgin fossil-fuel-derived resin); designing for disassembly to enable material recovery at end of life. Each strategy should be evaluated for both effectiveness and limitations.

title: Textiles
objectives: Classify textiles as natural, synthetic, or regenerated and give examples of each.
Describe the properties and applications of key textile fibres including cotton, wool, polyester, and nylon.
Explain how the structure of a textile (fibre, yarn, and fabric construction) affects its properties.
Analyse how textile finishing processes alter the performance of a fabric.
Discuss the environmental and social sustainability concerns associated with the textile industry.
summary:
Textiles are flexible materials consisting of fibres — either in their fibrous form (non-wovens) or twisted into yarns and then woven, knitted, or otherwise constructed into fabric. They encompass a vast range of products from clothing and home furnishings to technical textiles used in medical, aerospace, and construction applications. In IB D&T, textiles are studied as an engineering material group alongside metals, plastics, and composites.

Textile fibres are classified by origin. Natural fibres come from plant or animal sources: plant-based (cellulosic) fibres include cotton (from the seed boll of the cotton plant — soft, breathable, absorbent, comfortable next to skin, widely used in clothing and household textiles), linen (from the flax plant — strong, cool, crisp, used for summer clothing and table linen), and jute and hemp (natural fibre ropes, bags, and composite reinforcement). Animal fibres include wool (from sheep — excellent thermal insulation, moisture-wicking, naturally flame resistant, used in knitwear and carpets) and silk (from silkworm cocoons — strong, lustrous, smooth, expensive, used in luxury clothing).

Synthetic fibres are produced entirely from petrochemicals by polymerisation. Polyester (PET-based) is the world's most widely produced synthetic fibre — strong, dimensionally stable, resistant to stretching and shrinking, quick-drying, inexpensive, but less breathable than natural fibres and a major contributor to microplastic pollution. Nylon (polyamide) was the first fully synthetic fibre — high tensile strength, abrasion resistance, and elasticity; used in hosiery, ropes, and technical textiles. Acrylic (polyacrylonitrile) mimics wool in appearance and feel, is lightweight and warm, but pills and is not biodegradable. Lycra/Spandex (polyurethane elastane) provides exceptional stretch and recovery — mixed with other fibres in sportswear and underwear.

Regenerated (semi-synthetic) fibres are made from naturally occurring polymers (typically cellulose from wood pulp) that are dissolved and re-spun. Viscose (rayon) is soft and drapes well but has low wet strength. Modal and Lyocell (Tencel) are improved regenerated cellulose fibres produced by more sustainable closed-loop processes.

Fabric construction profoundly affects performance. Woven fabrics (interlacing warp and weft yarns) are dimensionally stable and do not stretch significantly on the bias; plain weave, twill (diagonal rib — denim), and satin are the main weave types. Knitted fabrics (interlocking loops) stretch considerably in multiple directions, providing comfort and fit — used in T-shirts, knitwear, and sportswear. Non-woven fabrics (fibres bonded by heat, chemicals, or mechanical interlocking without weaving or knitting) are used for disposable medical gowns, wipes, and insulation.

Finishing processes applied after weaving or knitting include: mercerisation (treating cotton with caustic soda to increase lustre and dye uptake); flame-retardant finishing (chemical treatments to slow ignition — mandatory for children's nightwear in many countries); waterproofing (DWR — durable water repellent coating); and anti-wrinkle/easy-care finishes (cross-linking cellulose chains in cotton). The environmental impact of the textile industry is substantial: it is among the most polluting industries globally, with significant water consumption and pollution from dyeing and finishing, greenhouse gas emissions from synthetic fibre production, and labour rights concerns across global supply chains.

The fast-fashion model — producing large volumes of low-cost, short-lived garments — exacerbates environmental and social problems. Sustainable design strategies include designing for longevity, use of organic or recycled fibres, waterless dyeing technologies, closed-loop recycling of garments, and fair trade certification ensuring living wages and safe conditions for workers.

quiz.question: Classify the following fibres as natural, synthetic, or regenerated: cotton, polyester, viscose, wool, nylon.
quiz.markscheme: Natural: cotton (plant/cellulosic), wool (animal). Synthetic: polyester (petrochemical), nylon (petrochemical). Regenerated: viscose (cellulose from wood pulp, dissolved and re-spun). Students should correctly categorise all five.

quiz.question: Explain why wool provides better thermal insulation than cotton, with reference to fibre structure.
quiz.markscheme: Wool fibres are crimped/wavy, which traps air within and between fibres — air is a poor thermal conductor, so trapped air provides insulation. Wool also wicks moisture away from the skin while maintaining insulating properties when damp (unlike cotton, which becomes cold and heavy when wet). Cotton fibres are smooth and straight, packing more densely and trapping less air.
