The moisture-absorbing (hygroscopic) nature of cotton makes it comfortable in warm conditions but less effective as thermal insulation.

quiz.question: Describe how woven and knitted fabric constructions differ, and explain the implication for their use in product design.
quiz.markscheme: Woven fabrics interlace warp and weft yarns at right angles — they are dimensionally stable and have little stretch except on the bias. Suitable for structured garments (shirts, trousers), upholstery, bags, and sails. Knitted fabrics are formed by interlocking loops — they stretch in multiple directions, providing comfort, fit, and recovery. Suitable for T-shirts, knitwear, sportswear, and any application requiring stretch or close body fit. The construction choice significantly affects drape, stretch, cutting, and sewing requirements.

quiz.question: Analyse the environmental impact of polyester as a fibre, including both production and end-of-life considerations.
quiz.markscheme: Production: polyester is derived from petrochemicals (fossil fuels), consuming energy and producing CO₂; the production process generates chemical waste. During use: synthetic fibres release microplastics during washing — these enter waterways and marine ecosystems, accumulating in food chains and organisms. End of life: polyester does not biodegrade on human timescales; mechanical recycling (from PET bottles or garments) is possible but quality and scale are limited; chemical recycling (depolymerisation) is emerging but not yet at industrial scale. Overall impact is high compared to natural fibres in most lifecycle categories, though durability may reduce replacement frequency.

quiz.question: Discuss two sustainability challenges in the global textile industry and suggest a design or business strategy to address each.
quiz.markscheme: Accept any two with discussion and a proposed strategy: water pollution from dyeing and finishing (address through waterless dyeing technologies — e.g. supercritical CO₂ dyeing — and mandatory effluent treatment regulations); microplastic pollution from synthetic fibres (address through filter washing bags, designing with natural/recycled fibres, or developing biodegradable synthetic fibres); labour rights abuses in low-cost production countries (address through fair trade certification, supply chain transparency, and living wage commitments); fast fashion waste (address through durability-focused design, garment take-back/recycling schemes, or rental/leasing business models). Each challenge should be explained and the strategy assessed for effectiveness.

title: Composites
objectives: Define a composite material and explain the roles of the matrix and reinforcement phases.
Describe the properties and applications of glass fibre reinforced polymer (GFRP) and carbon fibre reinforced polymer (CFRP).
Explain how the orientation of reinforcement fibres affects composite properties.
Analyse why composites offer advantages over conventional materials for specific applications.
Discuss the challenges of recycling composite materials.
summary:
A composite material consists of two or more constituent materials with distinctly different physical or chemical properties that, when combined, produce a material with characteristics different from (and often superior to) those of the individual components. The most common engineering composite structure consists of a reinforcement phase — which provides strength and stiffness — embedded in a matrix phase — which binds the reinforcement, transfers loads, and protects reinforcement fibres from damage. Neither phase could achieve the combined performance on its own.

Natural composites exist — wood (cellulose fibre reinforcement in a lignin matrix) and bone (hydroxyapatite mineral in a collagen matrix) are both composites — but engineered composites are designed to achieve specific property combinations. The most widely used engineered composites in product design are fibre-reinforced polymers (FRPs), in which continuous or chopped fibres are embedded in a polymer matrix (typically epoxy, polyester, or vinyl ester resin).

Glass fibre reinforced polymer (GFRP, fibreglass) uses glass fibres as reinforcement. Glass fibres are strong in tension, relatively inexpensive, and chemically resistant, though less stiff than carbon fibres. GFRP is used in boat hulls, wind turbine blades (smaller scales), automotive body panels, sports equipment, and building cladding. It is produced by hand lay-up (glass mat or woven glass fabric impregnated manually with resin — labour-intensive, moderate quality), spray-up (chopped glass and resin sprayed into a mould), or resin transfer moulding (RTM — dry preform placed in a mould and resin injected under pressure — better consistency).

Carbon fibre reinforced polymer (CFRP) uses carbon fibres as reinforcement. Carbon fibres have very high specific stiffness and strength — far superior to glass fibres and all metals on a per-unit-weight basis. CFRP is used in aerospace (fuselage panels, wing skins, tail sections), high-performance automotive (F1 monocoques, sports car body structures), sporting goods (bicycles, tennis rackets, golf clubs), and increasingly in wind turbine blades. CFRP is significantly more expensive than GFRP due to the cost of carbon fibre production (energy-intensive high-temperature pyrolysis process) and typically requires autoclave curing for aerospace-grade parts.

Fibre orientation critically affects composite properties. Unidirectional (UD) composites have all fibres aligned in one direction — maximum stiffness and strength in the fibre direction but poor transverse properties. This is appropriate when loads are known to act in one direction (e.g. the span direction of a wing spar). Woven fabrics have fibres in two directions (0° and 90°) — more balanced properties. Quasi-isotropic laminates (fibres at 0°, 45°, 90°, -45°) approach isotropic in-plane properties. The designer controls property direction by selecting fibre orientations for each ply in a laminate.

The primary recycling challenge for composites is that the thermoset matrix and fibre reinforcement are intimately bonded and cannot be separated by remelting. Current end-of-life options include: pyrolysis (burning the resin to recover carbon fibres — recovers fibres but degrades their properties); mechanical grinding (reduces to powder for use as filler — low value); cement co-processing (burning whole composites in cement kilns — energy recovery, waste disposal). True closed-loop recycling of high-quality carbon fibre composites remains a research and development challenge, making composites difficult to justify from a circular economy perspective despite their outstanding performance.

quiz.question: Define a composite material and explain the distinct roles of the matrix and reinforcement phases.
quiz.markscheme: A composite is a material made from two or more constituents with different properties, combined to produce a material with characteristics superior to those of either alone. The reinforcement (e.g. carbon or glass fibres) provides high strength and stiffness — it carries most of the applied load. The matrix (e.g. epoxy resin) binds the reinforcement together, transfers loads between fibres, protects fibres from surface damage and the environment, and gives the composite its shape. Neither component achieves the composite's combined properties independently.

quiz.question: Compare the properties and applications of GFRP and CFRP, explaining why CFRP is preferred for aerospace applications despite its higher cost.
quiz.markscheme: GFRP: glass fibre reinforcement in polymer matrix — high strength, moderate stiffness, relatively inexpensive, widely used in marine, automotive, and sports applications. CFRP: carbon fibre reinforcement — much higher specific stiffness and strength than GFRP and all structural metals, very low density. In aerospace, weight is critical — CFRP's superior specific stiffness and strength reduces structural weight, directly reducing fuel consumption and increasing payload. The cost premium is justified by the fuel savings over a long aircraft life. GFRP is inappropriate for most aerospace structural applications because its lower stiffness would result in unacceptable structural deformation.

quiz.question: Explain how fibre orientation in a composite laminate is used to optimise performance for a specific loading condition.
quiz.markscheme: Composites are anisotropic — properties vary with direction. In unidirectional (UD) composites, stiffness and strength are maximum along the fibre direction. A designer stacks plies with fibres oriented in directions corresponding to the expected load directions. For a beam loaded in one direction (e.g. a spar under bending), most plies run along the span. For a panel subject to biaxial loads, a quasi-isotropic layup (0°/±45°/90°) distributes strength in all in-plane directions. Tailoring fibre orientation is a powerful design tool that allows efficient use of the expensive carbon fibre.

quiz.question: Analyse three advantages that CFRP offers over aluminium alloy for the construction of a racing bicycle frame.
quiz.markscheme: CFRP advantages: higher specific stiffness — stiffer frame for the same weight, improving power transfer efficiency; higher specific strength — stronger structure at lower mass; ability to tailor anisotropic properties by fibre lay-up, so the frame is stiff in bending but can be designed for some compliance in the vertical direction for rider comfort; excellent fatigue resistance under cyclic loading. CFRP limitation: more expensive, cannot be easily repaired after crash damage (aluminium can be welded), and end-of-life recycling is challenging compared to aluminium (which is infinitely recyclable).

quiz.question: Discuss the challenges of recycling fibre-reinforced polymer composites and evaluate the current end-of-life options.
quiz.markscheme: The thermoset matrix is irreversibly cross-linked — it cannot be remelted to separate it from fibres. Current options: pyrolysis (burns the resin, recovers fibres with some degradation of properties — recovered short fibres have lower value than virgin fibre); mechanical grinding (low-value filler material); cement kiln co-processing (energy recovery and waste disposal but material value lost). None approach closed-loop recycling. This is a significant sustainability concern as composite use grows in aerospace and wind energy — large volumes of end-of-life composite waste are anticipated. Thermoplastic matrix composites (TPCs) offer improved recyclability but have not yet matched the performance of thermoset systems for demanding structural applications. Overall: current recycling options are inadequate and this remains an active area of research.

title: Scales of production
objectives: Identify and distinguish between the main scales of production: one-off, batch, mass, and continuous.
Explain how the scale of production influences tooling investment, unit cost, and design decisions.
Describe the characteristics of one-off (bespoke) production and give examples.
Analyse the advantages and limitations of mass production compared to batch production.
Discuss how the choice of production scale affects material selection and product design.
summary:
The scale at which a product is manufactured is a fundamental design and business decision that affects material choice, manufacturing process selection, tooling investment, unit cost, quality consistency, and the overall product design. IB Design & Technology identifies four main scales of production: one-off, batch, mass, and continuous.

One-off (job or bespoke) production involves making a single unique item to individual specifications. Examples include a custom-designed piece of furniture, a bridge, a bespoke wedding dress, or a film set. Because no production run is involved, no expensive tooling or jigs are justified — production relies heavily on skilled craft labour and general-purpose tools. Unit cost is high because all fixed costs and labour are absorbed by a single item. Quality can be very high because skilled craftspeople can adjust and rectify as they work. One-off production is appropriate for highly customised, high-value products where volume does not justify tooling investment.

Batch production involves making a predetermined quantity (a batch) of identical products, after which the production facility may be reconfigured to make a different batch. Examples include a print run of 500 books, a batch of hand-made chocolates, or a limited run of precision engineering components. Jigs and fixtures are used to ensure consistency within the batch. Between batches, the production facility is set up differently (set-up cost). Batch production offers moderate economies of scale compared to one-off but is less efficient than mass production. It is suitable for moderate volumes of products with relatively frequent design changes.

Mass (flow) production involves making very large quantities of standardised products continuously using purpose-built production lines with high levels of mechanisation and automation. Classic examples include the production of cars, consumer electronics, and soft drinks bottles. High tooling and plant investment is justified because the cost is spread over a very large number of units, driving unit cost very low. The product design is standardised and fixed for the production run; design changes are expensive because they require retooling. Quality is controlled through automated monitoring rather than individual craftsmanship. Workers typically perform narrow, repetitive tasks.

Continuous production is similar to mass production but involves truly uninterrupted flow 24 hours per day, typically for commodity materials or chemical products — oil refining, steel production, paper mills, and electricity generation. The product is homogeneous and undifferentiated; the plant runs continuously because shut-down and start-up costs are very high.

The scale of production profoundly influences product design. For one-off products, the designer can specify materials and processes that would be uneconomic at volume (e.g. hand-finished hardwood, hand-welded steel). For mass production, the designer must select materials suitable for high-speed automated processing (e.g. injection-mouldable polymers, press-stampable sheet steel), minimise the number of parts and assembly operations, and design features that facilitate automated assembly. Injection mould tooling for a complex plastic component can cost tens of thousands to hundreds of thousands of pounds — this investment is only justified if the production volume is sufficient.

quiz.question: Distinguish between batch production and mass production, giving one example of a product suited to each.
quiz.markscheme: Batch production: a predetermined number of identical items are made, after which the facility is reconfigured — moderate volume, product may change between runs. Example: a limited run of precision machine components, a print run of textbooks. Mass production: large volumes of standardised products are made continuously on dedicated production lines with high automation — very high volume, standardised product, low unit cost. Example: production of cars, consumer electronics, PET bottles. Key distinctions: volume (higher in mass production), tooling investment, unit cost, flexibility, and the ability to change product design.

quiz.question: Explain how the scale of production affects the design of a product's joining methods.
quiz.markscheme: In one-off production, time-consuming but strong joints (mortise and tenon in timber, hand-welded metal) are appropriate because labour cost is absorbed by one item and skill is available. In batch production, jigs and fixtures ensure repeatable joints; MIG or spot welding may be used. In mass production, joints must be capable of automated assembly: snap-fit plastic joints, crimping, resistance spot welding, or adhesive bonding applied by robot are chosen to minimise assembly time. Fasteners that require individual tightening are avoided in high-volume production because they are time-limiting. The joint design must match the production process.

quiz.question: Analyse why injection moulding is only economically viable above a certain production volume.
quiz.markscheme: Injection moulding requires a steel mould (tool) that must be precision-machined to the exact geometry of the part — this can cost £10,000 to £100,000+. This is a fixed cost that must be amortised over the production run. At low volumes (e.g. 100 units), the mould cost per part is prohibitive. As volume increases, the mould cost per part falls rapidly (economies of scale), and the very low cycle time and material waste of injection moulding make unit cost very competitive. The break-even volume (compared to, say, 3D printing) depends on part complexity and material, but is typically in the thousands to tens of thousands of units.

quiz.question: Describe the characteristics of one-off production and explain why it typically results in higher unit costs than mass production.
quiz.markscheme: One-off production makes a single unique item to individual specifications, relying on skilled craft labour and general-purpose tools with no dedicated tooling or automation. All fixed costs (design time, set-up, materials) are absorbed by a single unit. Labour time per unit is high because craftspeople must adapt and adjust without jigs or automated guidance. There are no economies of scale in material purchasing. In contrast, mass production spreads high tooling investment over many units, uses low-cost automated operations at high speed, and benefits from bulk material purchasing — driving unit cost far below one-off levels.

quiz.question: Discuss how the scale of production should influence a designer's choice of material and surface finish for a children's toy.
quiz.markscheme: At mass production scale (millions of units): injection moulding thermoplastics (ABS, PP) is appropriate — materials are processable at high speed, moulds produce integral surface texture and colour, no secondary finishing needed, and unit cost is low. Paints and coatings should be in-mould or applied robotically. At low volume/one-off: hand-shaped and finished timber or hand-cast resin may be appropriate — processes unavailable at mass volume. Safety standards (EN 71 in Europe) apply regardless of scale. The designer must match material, surface finish process, and cost to the planned production volume from the outset — specifying a mass production design process for a low-volume product would be uneconomic.

title: Manufacturing processes
objectives: Classify manufacturing processes into forming, cutting/wasting, joining, and finishing categories.
Describe key forming processes including casting, forging, extrusion, and injection moulding.
Describe key cutting/wasting processes including turning, milling, drilling, and laser cutting.
Explain how joining processes are selected based on material type and design requirements.
Analyse how the choice of manufacturing process constrains or enables specific product designs.
summary:
Manufacturing processes are the methods by which raw materials are converted into finished products or components. They are fundamental to product design because the design of every feature — every hole, every surface, every joint — must be achievable by a practical manufacturing process. Designers must understand manufacturing processes not merely as a list to know, but as a set of capabilities and constraints that shape design decisions from the earliest stages.

Manufacturing processes are commonly classified into four categories. Forming processes give a material its shape without removing significant material. These include: casting (pouring molten material into a mould — e.g. sand casting for grey iron engine blocks, die casting for aluminium components, injection moulding for thermoplastics, and slip casting for ceramics); forging (compressing metal between dies — hot or cold — producing parts with excellent grain flow and mechanical properties, e.g. connecting rods, crankshafts, hand tools); extrusion (forcing material through a die to produce a continuous profile — e.g. aluminium window frames, plastic pipe, rubber seals); rolling (reducing metal thickness between powered rollers — sheet metal, structural sections); drawing (pulling sheet through a die — deep drawing produces cup-shaped forms such as drink cans and car door panels); and thermoforming (heating thermoplastic sheet and forming it over a mould by vacuum or pressure — food packaging, baths, vehicle interior panels).

Cutting and wasting (subtractive) processes remove material to achieve the required shape. Traditional machining processes include turning (rotating the workpiece against a fixed cutting tool on a lathe — produces cylindrical forms); milling (rotating cutter traverses the workpiece — produces flat surfaces, slots, and complex 3D profiles); drilling (rotating drill bit removes material to produce circular holes); and grinding (abrasive wheel removes very small amounts of material to very tight tolerances and fine surface finish). Non-traditional cutting processes include laser cutting (focused laser beam melts/vaporises material — fast, precise, no cutting forces, suitable for thin sheet metal, wood, and acrylic), waterjet cutting, and electrical discharge machining (EDM).

Joining processes permanently or semi-permanently unite components. Permanent mechanical joining includes riveting and crimping. Fusion welding melts the parent metal and filler to form a metallurgical bond (MIG, TIG, spot welding). Adhesive bonding uses polymer adhesives for metals, glass, and composites. Brazing and soldering use a lower-melting-point filler. Screwing, bolting, and snap-fitting provide removable joints suitable for maintenance and disassembly.

Finishing processes improve the aesthetic appearance, corrosion resistance, or surface functional properties of a component. They include painting (brush, spray, electrostatic, powder coating), anodising (electrochemical oxidation of aluminium surface — hard, corrosion resistant, can be dyed), electroplating (depositing a metallic coating by electrolysis — chrome, nickel, zinc plating), polishing, and surface texturing.

The choice of manufacturing process is constrained by material type (not all processes work for all materials), production volume (some processes require expensive tooling justified only at high volume), geometric complexity (some complex forms can only be made by certain processes), dimensional accuracy requirements, and cost. Designers who understand these constraints can design features that are manufacturable and cost-effective, avoiding forms that would be unnecessarily expensive or technically difficult to produce.

quiz.question: Describe the injection moulding process and identify two design features that are required for successful injection-moulded parts.
quiz.markscheme: Injection moulding: molten thermoplastic is injected under high pressure into a closed steel mould, where it cools and solidifies before the mould opens and the part is ejected. Process is fast and highly repeatable; suitable for complex 3D forms at high volumes. Two required design features: draft angles (slight taper on vertical walls to allow the part to be ejected from the mould without sticking); uniform wall thickness (avoids differential cooling, which causes sink marks and warpage); also accept: no undercuts unless side actions are used; adequate radii at corners; gate location considerations.

quiz.question: Explain the difference between a forming process and a wasting (subtractive) process, and give one example of each.
quiz.markscheme: A forming process shapes material without removing significant amounts of it — e.g. forging squeezes metal between dies; extrusion forces material through a die. Material is displaced or flows into the new shape. A wasting (subtractive) process removes material to reveal the required shape — e.g. turning removes material from a rotating workpiece using a cutting tool; milling cuts slots or profiles. Forming tends to waste less material; subtractive processes can produce more precise features but generate swarf/waste material.

quiz.question: Describe MIG welding and explain what material types it is suitable for.
quiz.markscheme: MIG (Metal Inert Gas) welding, also called GMAW (Gas Metal Arc Welding), uses a continuously fed consumable wire electrode as both the arc source and filler metal. A shielding gas (argon, CO₂, or a mixture) protects the weld pool from atmospheric oxidation. It produces a weld pool that fuses the parent metals together. MIG welding is suitable for steel (mild, low-alloy, and stainless), aluminium alloys, and some other metals. It is widely used in automotive manufacturing, fabrication workshops, and construction. It is not suitable for non-metallic materials (plastics, composites, ceramics). Aluminium MIG welding requires different wire and shielding gas.

quiz.question: Analyse how the choice of manufacturing process affects the design of internal features such as holes and cavities.
quiz.markscheme: Some internal features can only be produced by specific processes: a circular through-hole can be drilled; a complex internal passage requires casting with a core or investment casting; an undercut feature in a moulded plastic part requires a side-action in the mould (increasing cost and complexity). Laser cutting can produce internal profiles in sheet material but not in solid 3D forms. Blind holes (non-through) cannot be produced by standard casting without a core. Designers must therefore ensure that every internal feature in their design is producible by an available process at acceptable cost, which sometimes means simplifying the geometry or changing the manufacturing process.

quiz.question: Describe two surface finishing processes and explain the functional benefit each provides.
quiz.markscheme: Accept any two: anodising aluminium (electrochemical process that grows a thick, hard aluminium oxide layer on the surface — significantly improves corrosion and scratch resistance; can be dyed for aesthetics — used on bicycle components, architectural aluminium); powder coating (dry paint powder electrostatically applied then cured at ~180°C — produces a hard, durable, corrosion-resistant coating in a wide colour range without solvent emissions — used on garden furniture, car wheels, steel frames); chrome electroplating (deposits a hard, bright chromium layer — aesthetic and wear resistance — used on plumbing fittings and automotive trim); hot-dip galvanising (coating steel with zinc by immersion in molten zinc — sacrificial cathodic protection against corrosion — used on structural steelwork, fence posts, bolts).

title: Production systems
objectives: Describe the main types of production systems: project, jobbing, batch, flow/mass, and continuous.
Explain the concept of a production line and describe its key features.
Describe the principles of flexible manufacturing systems (FMS).
Analyse how automation and mechanisation change the nature of production systems.
Discuss the social implications of automation in manufacturing.
summary:
A production system is the organised combination of people, equipment, materials, and processes used to manufacture products. Matching the production system to the product type, volume, and variety is a key engineering and management decision. Different production systems suit different combinations of product variety and volume.

Project production is used for large, unique, immobile products such as buildings, bridges, ships, and power stations. Resources (labour, equipment, materials) are brought to the product location and the project is managed as a unique one-off endeavour. Jobbing production involves skilled workers making individual items to customer specification in a workshop, typically using general-purpose equipment — examples include bespoke tool-making, specialist repair work, and custom furniture manufacture. Batch production, as discussed in topic 4.8, groups production into defined batches of identical items, with set-up changes between batches. Flow (mass) production organises manufacturing as a continuous sequence of operations through which products move — the production line. Continuous production runs without interruption, day and night, for commodity products.

The production line is the defining structure of mass production. Products move through a series of workstations in a fixed sequence; each station performs a specific operation (an assembly step, a machining operation, a test). The rate at which products move through the line is called the cycle time; the critical constraint is the slowest (bottleneck) station, which determines the maximum throughput. Henry Ford's introduction of the moving assembly line in 1913 was the foundational innovation of mass production, enabling dramatic reductions in assembly time and cost for the Model T Ford.

Mechanisation replaces human muscle effort with machines but still requires human operators to control and direct operations. Automation replaces human decision-making and control as well, using sensors, control systems, and programmed instructions. Fixed automation (hard automation) uses dedicated equipment configured for one specific task — highly efficient but inflexible. Programmable automation (e.g. CNC machines, robots) can be reprogrammed to perform different operations, enabling greater variety at some cost in efficiency. Flexible manufacturing systems (FMS) combine CNC machine tools, automated material handling (conveyor systems, AGVs), and computer control into an integrated system capable of producing a variety of different parts automatically, switching between part types without manual intervention. FMS offers the efficiency benefits of automation with the variety benefits of batch production — a key competitive advantage in markets demanding variety.

Automation raises significant social questions. It displaces human workers from routine manufacturing tasks, with the potential for structural unemployment in regions dependent on manufacturing employment. However, it also creates new forms of skilled work (programming, maintenance, engineering) and may move humans away from dangerous, unhealthy, or repetitive tasks. The distribution of economic gains from automation — whether benefits flow to shareholders, workers, or consumers — is a contested political and economic issue. Designers must be aware that their design decisions (choosing to design for automated assembly, or selecting components that eliminate manual operations) have social as well as technical consequences.

quiz.question: Distinguish between jobbing production and flow (mass) production, explaining the type of product each system is best suited to.
quiz.markscheme: Jobbing production: skilled workers make individual items to customer specification in a workshop, using general-purpose equipment — suited to low-volume, high-variety, customised products (e.g. bespoke tools, specialist repair). Flow (mass) production: large volumes of standardised products pass through a fixed sequence of operations on a production line — suited to high-volume, standardised products where low unit cost is essential (e.g. consumer electronics, automobiles). The key distinctions are volume, variety, skill level required, and unit cost.

quiz.question: Explain the concept of a bottleneck in a production line and describe its effect on throughput.
quiz.markscheme: A bottleneck is the slowest workstation in a production line — it has the longest cycle time and therefore limits the rate at which the whole line can produce finished products. No matter how fast other stations are, the line can only produce at the rate of the bottleneck. Throughput is determined by the bottleneck. Solutions: increase the capacity of the bottleneck (additional equipment, overtime), redesign the operation to reduce its time, or balance the line so all stations have similar cycle times. Identifying and eliminating bottlenecks is a core principle of lean manufacturing.

quiz.question: Describe a flexible manufacturing system (FMS) and explain how it combines the benefits of automation and batch production.
quiz.markscheme: An FMS integrates CNC machine tools, automated material handling (AGVs, conveyors, robotic loaders), and a central computer control system. The computer controls routing and scheduling of different parts through the machines. Part programs are stored and loaded automatically. The system can produce a family of different parts without manual changeover — switching between variants under computer control. This combines the efficiency and precision of automation (low labour cost, consistent quality, 24-hour operation) with the variety of batch production (multiple part types), making it suitable for mid-volume production of varied products, such as aerospace components or automotive transmission parts.

quiz.question: Analyse how increasing automation in manufacturing affects the skills required of the workforce.
quiz.markscheme: Automation reduces the demand for unskilled and semi-skilled manual labour (repetitive assembly, basic machining). It increases demand for higher-level skills: CNC programming, robot cell commissioning and maintenance, system integration, quality control data analysis, and manufacturing engineering. Workers must be more technically educated and adaptable. However, the number of high-skill jobs created may be smaller than the number of manual jobs eliminated, particularly in regions with high concentrations of manufacturing employment. There is a risk of skills polarisation — a growing gap between high-skill/high-pay and low-skill/low-pay work, with fewer mid-level skilled manufacturing jobs.

quiz.question: Discuss one social implication of increasing automation in manufacturing and suggest how governments or designers might respond.
quiz.markscheme: Structural unemployment: automation displaces workers in routine manufacturing roles — whole regions dependent on manufacturing face economic decline (as seen in de-industrialised areas in the UK, USA, and Europe). Government responses: investment in retraining and education to develop skills relevant to automated manufacturing; social safety nets; industrial strategy to attract new industries. Designer responses: designers can influence the degree of automation they design for, and the IB D&T framework asks designers to consider the social impact of their decisions. Full automation is not always the most socially responsible choice — hybrid human-machine systems may preserve employment while improving productivity. Automation should be evaluated against a range of criteria including social impact, not only economic efficiency.

title: Robots in automated production
objectives: Define an industrial robot and describe its key structural components.
Describe the main types of industrial robots and their applications.
Explain how robots are programmed for manufacturing tasks.
Analyse the advantages and limitations of using industrial robots in manufacturing.
Discuss the impact of robotics on manufacturing competitiveness and employment.
summary:
An industrial robot is a reprogrammable, multi-functional manipulator designed to move materials, parts, tools, or specialised devices through variable programmed motions for the performance of a variety of tasks. Industrial robots are a central technology of modern automated manufacturing and are found in automotive assembly plants, electronics factories, warehouses, food processing facilities, and increasingly in small and medium enterprises.

A typical industrial robot consists of: a manipulator arm — a series of rigid links connected by joints (articulations), typically configured as a six-axis (six degrees of freedom) serial kinematic chain similar to a human arm; a wrist assembly at the end of the arm that provides additional degrees of freedom for tool orientation; an end effector (end-of-arm tooling, EOAT) — the device attached at the wrist that interacts with the workpiece (e.g. a welding torch, gripper, spray gun, or drill); a power supply (electric servo motors are standard in modern robots); and a controller — the computer system that stores programs and executes robot motions.

Common robot configurations include: articulated robots (six-axis arms — the most versatile, capable of reaching any position and orientation in their work envelope — used for welding, assembly, and material handling); SCARA robots (Selective Compliance Articulated Robot Arm — designed for high-speed pick-and-place operations in a horizontal plane, used extensively in electronics assembly); delta robots (parallel kinematic structure, very high speed, low payload — used in packaging and food sorting); and Cartesian/gantry robots (linear axes in X, Y, Z — simple control, used for dispensing, milling, and 3D printing at large scale).

Robots can be programmed by several methods: teach pendant programming (an operator physically guides the robot end effector through the required motions using a handheld pendant, recording positions and sequences); offline programming (the robot program is written using simulation software modelling the robot and its environment, without stopping production — increasingly standard for complex tasks); and increasingly, vision-guided and AI-assisted programming where robots adapt their behaviour based on sensor feedback.

Advantages of industrial robots include: high speed and productivity; consistency and accuracy — robots perform the same motion repeatedly to within fractions of a millimetre; ability to work continuously without rest, in dangerous, hot, or toxic environments; reprogrammability — a robot can be retrained for different tasks; and improved product quality through consistent, controlled processes. Limitations include: high capital cost; the need for precisely controlled, structured environments (traditional robots cannot cope with variability); programming complexity; the requirement for safety barriers (though collaborative robots — cobots — are designed to work safely alongside humans without fixed guards); and maintenance requirements.

Robotics contributes to manufacturing competitiveness by lowering unit cost, improving quality consistency, and enabling complex operations that would be difficult for humans. However, robot adoption displaces human workers, contributing to structural unemployment in traditional manufacturing regions. The rise of collaborative robots (cobots) designed for human-robot collaboration rather than replacement introduces a new paradigm where automation can augment human capabilities rather than simply displace them.

quiz.question: Define an industrial robot and describe three of its key structural components.
quiz.markscheme: An industrial robot is a reprogrammable multi-functional manipulator for moving materials, parts, or tools through programmed motions. Key structural components: manipulator arm (series of rigid links connected by joints providing degrees of freedom); end effector (tool attached at the wrist that interacts with the workpiece — gripper, welding torch, spray gun); controller (computer system storing and executing motion programs); power supply (electric servo motors drive the joints). Students should describe the function of each component, not just name it.

quiz.question: Describe a SCARA robot and explain why it is particularly suited to electronics assembly operations.
quiz.markscheme: A SCARA (Selective Compliance Articulated Robot Arm) has two horizontal revolute joints (providing motion in the horizontal plane) and one vertical linear axis. It is selectively compliant — stiff in the vertical direction (resisting vertical forces) but compliant in the horizontal plane (allowing some flexibility). This makes it ideal for inserting components vertically into circuit boards (vertical stiffness ensures accurate placement; compliance helps components seat into sockets). SCARA robots are very fast (high-speed pick-and-place), accurate, and well-suited to the repetitive, high-volume, planar assembly operations of electronics manufacturing.

quiz.question: Explain the teach pendant programming method for industrial robots, including one advantage and one limitation.
quiz.markscheme: Teach pendant programming: an operator physically moves the robot through the required path using a handheld pendant (joystick or touchscreen) and records positions and sequences at each step. The robot then replays the taught sequence. Advantage: intuitive, does not require specialist robot programming knowledge — an operator with limited programming background can teach simple tasks. Limitation: requires the robot to be taken offline during programming (production stops); difficult to achieve precise geometric paths; not suitable for complex multi-axis motions or large programs. Offline programming (using simulation software) is preferred for complex tasks and avoids production downtime.

quiz.question: Analyse three advantages of using industrial robots over human workers for automotive welding operations.
quiz.markscheme: Speed: robots weld faster and more consistently than humans, increasing production rate; Accuracy and consistency: robots repeat the same weld path to within fractions of a millimetre, producing consistent weld quality regardless of time of day, fatigue, or operator variability — critical for structural welds where quality is safety-critical; Environment: welding involves intense heat, UV radiation, fumes, and sparks — conditions that are harmful to human health and require extensive PPE; robots can work in these conditions continuously without health risk; Reprogrammability: a robot can be retrained for different car models or weld patterns when the product changes, unlike fixed automation. Students should relate each advantage to the specific context of automotive welding.

quiz.question: Discuss how collaborative robots (cobots) differ from traditional industrial robots and evaluate their potential to change the relationship between automation and employment.
quiz.markscheme: Traditional industrial robots operate in safety-fenced cells separated from humans — they are fast and powerful but dangerous if humans enter the workspace. Cobots are designed to work safely alongside humans without fixed guards — they use force-limiting technology, light sensors, and low mass to detect and respond to human contact safely. This enables human-robot collaboration: the robot handles heavy, repetitive, or precise operations while the human provides adaptability, problem-solving, and dexterity for complex tasks. Employment implications: cobots can augment human workers (increasing productivity without full replacement) rather than displacing them entirely, potentially offering a more socially sustainable automation model. Limitations: cobots are slower than traditional robots (force limits reduce speed) and more expensive relative to capability. Their impact on employment depends on how businesses choose to deploy them — augmentation or replacement.
